Distress Tolerance Handout 18a   (Distress Tolerance Worksheet 16)
Behavior Patterns Characteristic of Addict Mind
and of Clean Mind
Addict Mind

¬â Engaging in addictive behavior.

¬â Thinking, ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt really have a problem with
addiction.‚Äù

¬â Thinking, ‚ÄúI can do a little.‚Äù

¬â Thinking, ‚ÄúI can indulge my habit, if only on
weekends.‚Äù

¬â Thinking, ‚ÄúI can‚Äôt stand this!‚Äù

¬â Glamorizing addiction.

¬â Surfing the Internet for ways to engage in
addictive behaviors.

¬â Buying paraphernalia (food, drugs, videos,
etc.) for addictive behavior.

¬â Selling or exchanging items related to
addictive behaviors.

¬â Stealing to pay for addiction.

¬â Prostituting for money or for paraphernalia.

¬â Lying.

¬â Hiding.

¬â Isolating.

¬â Acting always busy; ‚ÄúGot to go!‚Äù

¬â Breaking promises.

¬â Committing crimes.

¬â Acting like a corpse.

¬â Having ‚Äúno life.‚Äù

¬â Acting desperate/obsessed.

¬â Not looking people in the eyes.

¬â Having poor hygiene.

¬â Avoiding doctors.

¬â Other:

Clean Mind

¬â Engaging in apparently irrelevant behaviors
that in the past inevitably led to addictive
behavior.

¬â Thinking, ‚ÄúI‚Äôve learned my lesson.‚Äù

¬â Thinking, ‚ÄúI can control the habit.‚Äù

¬â Thinking, ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt really have an addiction
problem any more.‚Äù

¬â Stopping or cutting back medication that helps
with addiction.

¬â Being in environments where others engage in
addictive behaviors.

¬â Seeing friends who are still addicted.

¬â Living with people who are addicted.

¬â Keeping addiction paraphernalia.

¬â Carrying around extra money.

¬â Being irresponsible with bills.

¬â Dressing like an addict.

¬â Not going to meetings.

¬â Not confronting the problems that fuel my
addictive behaviors.

¬â Acting as if only willpower is needed.

¬â Isolating.

¬â Believing, ‚ÄúI can do this alone.‚Äù

¬â Thinking, ‚ÄúI can take pain medicine/diet/
engage in addictive behavior if prescribed or
advised; I don‚Äôt need to say anything about my
past addiction.‚Äù

¬â Thinking, ‚ÄúI can‚Äôt stand this!‚Äù


ourspace_shared_life_filter.title: Behavior Patterns Characteristic of Addict Mind and of Clean Mind Addict Mind 
ourspace_shared_life_filter.shared_facing_summary: Distress tolerance means getting through the wave without adding harm. Use gentle, medically safer versions first.
tiny_steps: Choose one body-safe skill for 30 seconds.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

accessibility_modifications: Replace intense exercise with hand squeeze, paced exhale, safe fidget, weighted comfort if tolerated, or visual grounding.
Avoid cold shock/breath-holding/intense exercise unless already clinician-approved and safe that day.
Keep oxygen, rescue meds, allergy plan, mobility aids, and exit routes accessible.
relationship_and_caregiver_notes: William's needs and Jasper's capacity both count.
Ask before helping; ask before escalating; name the smallest next step.
Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
identity_and_trauma_filter: Use William's masculine language and Jasper's they/them language.
Use consent-based, non-shaming, body-neutral language unless a person has explicitly chosen a term.
Do not turn symptoms, dysphoria, trauma responses, clutter, fatigue, or emotional intensity into moral failure.
avoid_or_adjust: Do not use crisis skills as punishment, sensory flooding, or exposure without consent.
